model_narrative: We are making a metal blend by buying and mixing several candidate alloys. Each alloy has a known composition telling how much of each element it contributes, and a known price per pound. We decide how much of each alloy to buy. The objective is to make the total purchase cost as small as possible.

sets.name: alloy
sets.doc: the candidate alloys that can be bought and mixed into the blend

sets.name: elem
sets.doc: the chemical elements whose content in the final blend is controlled

params.name: compdat
params.doc: for each alloy, the percentage of each element it contains and, under the extra label 'price', its purchase price in dollars per pound. The element rows give the percent by weight of that element in the alloy

params.name: rb
params.doc: the required amount of each element in the final blend, expressed on the same percent-by-weight basis as the alloy compositions

vars.name: v
vars.doc: the amount of each alloy purchased, in pounds

vars.name: phi
vars.doc: total cost of the alloys bought, in dollars

constraints.name: compdat[elem,
constraints.intent: For each element, the blend has to hit its required amount. Add up the contribution of that element from every alloy, weighting each alloy by how much of it is bought, and that total must equal the amount of the element the blend is required to have.

constraints.name: ac
constraints.intent: The total cost is the price of each alloy multiplied by how much of it is bought, summed over all the alloys. Set the total cost equal to that amount.

query: Procurement wants to ease our dependence on alloy d — the premium lead-rich item we already lean on. There's a proposal that our d purchase shouldn't run more than double our c purchase going forward. What does the optimizer return for the minimum blend cost under that rule?

model_narrative: We are making a metal blend by buying and mixing several candidate alloys. Each alloy has a known composition telling how much of each element it contributes, and a known price per pound. We decide how much of each alloy to buy. The objective is to make the total purchase cost as small as possible.

sets.name: alloy
sets.doc: the candidate alloys that can be bought and mixed into the blend

sets.name: elem
sets.doc: the chemical elements whose content in the final blend is controlled

params.name: compdat
params.doc: for each alloy, the percentage of each element it contains and, under the extra label 'price', its purchase price in dollars per pound. The element rows give the percent by weight of that element in the alloy

params.name: rb
params.doc: the required amount of each element in the final blend, expressed on the same percent-by-weight basis as the alloy compositions

vars.name: v
vars.doc: the amount of each alloy purchased, in pounds

vars.name: phi
vars.doc: total cost of the alloys bought, in dollars

constraints.name: compdat[elem,
constraints.intent: For each element, the blend has to hit its required amount. Add up the contribution of that element from every alloy, weighting each alloy by how much of it is bought, and that total must equal the amount of the element the blend is required to have.

constraints.name: ac
constraints.intent: The total cost is the price of each alloy multiplied by how much of it is bought, summed over all the alloys. Set the total cost equal to that amount.

query: We're evaluating a supplier-diversification policy: keep at least 5% of the total buy, by weight, sitting in the premium alloys e through i so we're not entirely reliant on the bargain tier. Plug that in and tell me where the blend cost lands.
expected_pyomo: model.premium_floor = Constraint(expr=model.v['e'] + model.v['f'] + model.v['g'] + model.v['h'] + model.v['i'] >= 0.05 * sum(model.v[a] for a in model.alloy))

constraints.name: ac
constraints.intent: The total cost is the price of each alloy multiplied by how much of it is bought, summed over all the alloys. Set the total cost equal to that amount.

query: There's a proposal to keep at least 10% of the total purchase, by weight, in alloy a so we maintain a standing relationship with that supplier. If we hold alloy a to no less than a tenth of the buy, what does the optimizer return for the blend cost?
expected_pyomo: model.alloy_a_floor = Constraint(expr=model.v['a'] >= 0.10 * sum(model.v[aa] for aa in model.alloy))

query: The current plan is heavily concentrated on just two alloys. Wouldn't it be safer if no single alloy made up more than 30% of total purchase by weight? What's keeping the model from spreading the buy out?

query: Alloys a and b are the two cheapest on our price list. Couldn't we require those two combined to make up at least 60% of total purchase by weight? Leaning on the cheap end should beat the current plan — what's the model seeing that says otherwise?
expected_pyomo: model.cheap_floor = Constraint(expr=model.v['a'] + model.v['b'] >= 0.6 * sum(model.v[a] for a in model.alloy))

query: We're leaning hard on alloy d — it's a big slice of the buy. Shouldn't we cap it at a quarter of total purchase so one supplier can't strand us? What's keeping the model from trimming d back that far?
expected_pyomo: model.cap_d = Constraint(expr=model.v['d'] <= 0.25 * sum(model.v[a] for a in model.alloy))

query: Alloy a is the single cheapest item we can buy, yet the plan orders none of it. Shouldn't we force at least 20% of the total buy onto alloy a? What's keeping the model from using the bargain alloy at all?
expected_pyomo: model.use_a = Constraint(expr=model.v['a'] >= 0.20 * sum(model.v[a] for a in model.alloy))
